lesson.title: TIG Welding (GTAW)
lesson.explanation: Gas Tungsten Arc Welding produces the highest quality welds. Non-consumable tungsten electrode creates the arc; filler rod added separately. Shielding: pure argon. Ideal for stainless steel, aluminium, thin materials, and pipe work. Requires both hands (torch + filler) and foot pedal for amperage. Slowest process but best appearance and strength. Used in aerospace, food processing (sanitary welds), and artistic metalwork. In SA: high demand for TIG welders in petrochemical and food industries.

practice_problems.question: Specify the complete TIG setup for welding 2mm 304 stainless steel butt joint: tungsten, filler, gas, polarity, amperage. Why is TIG preferred over MIG for thin stainless?
practice_problems.answer: Tungsten: 2% lanthanated (blue), 1.6mm. Filler: ER308L, 1.6mm. Gas: 100% Ar at 8-10 L/min. Polarity: DCEN. Amperage: 40-60A with foot pedal. Back-purge inside with argon. TIG preferred because: lower minimum heat (prevents burn-through), precise foot pedal control, zero spatter (critical for food/medical), superior appearance, better gas coverage for corrosion resistance.
hints: DCEN: 70% heat to workpiece, 30% to tungsten.
Filler should match or over-alloy the base (308L for 304).
practice_problems.explanation: TIG thin stainless is a high-value skill for SA food, pharmaceutical, and architectural industries.

practice_problems.question: Why does aluminium TIG welding require AC polarity? Explain the 'cleaning action' and why it is essential.
practice_problems.answer: AC is required because each half-cycle serves a different purpose: DCEN half = penetration and heat. DCEP half = cleaning action (positive electrode attracts electrons from aluminium surface, breaking up the Al2O3 oxide layer). Cleaning is essential because aluminium oxide melts at 2072°C while aluminium melts at 660°C -- without removing oxide, filler cannot fuse to the base metal. AC balance control adjusts ratio: more EN = deeper penetration; more EP = more cleaning. Start at 70/30. Use pure argon, pure or zirconiated tungsten (forms a ball on tip, which is normal for AC).
hints: The oxide is invisible but reforms within seconds of removal.
If the pool has a dull 'skin', increase EP balance.
practice_problems.explanation: Understanding the oxide layer is the key to all aluminium welding. Every setup choice relates to managing this oxide.
distractors: Use DCEN for aluminium like steel
Thoriated tungsten is best for aluminium

practice_problems.question: TIG welding a stainless pipe, you notice dark blue/black discolouration inside the pipe at the root. Diagnose and explain the prevention method.
practice_problems.answer: Diagnosis: oxidation from lack of back-purging. The heat tint colour indicates severity: straw=light, blue/black=heavy oxidation with chromium depletion (destroys corrosion resistance). Prevention: BACK-PURGE -- seal pipe ends with foam dams, fill interior with argon before welding. Maintain purge until pipe cools below 300°C. For critical work, use an oxygen analyser (target <50 ppm O2). If oxidation occurred on a critical joint, either grind out the root and re-weld with purging, or pickle the pipe interior with acid.
hints: Heat tint chart: straw(300°C) → gold → brown → purple → blue → black(>700°C).
Food-grade rejects anything beyond light straw.
practice_problems.explanation: Back-purging separates qualified stainless pipe welders from general welders. Many SA food/beverage jobs require ASME-quality purged roots.
distractors: Blue colour indicates a strong weld

practice_problems.question: Your tungsten keeps getting contaminated (grey tip, erratic arc). List three common causes and describe the correct tungsten preparation procedure.
practice_problems.answer: Three causes: (1) Dipping tungsten into weld pool (base metal deposits on tip). (2) Filler rod touching tungsten. (3) Insufficient gas coverage causing oxidation. Preparation: (1) Cut off contaminated section with diamond disc. (2) Grind on a DEDICATED tungsten wheel (never use wheels that touched steel). (3) For DCEN: sharp point with LENGTHWISE grinding marks (crosswise marks make arc wander). (4) For AC: grind to a point, then briefly arc to form a ball. (5) Taper length: 2-3x electrode diameter.
hints: Grinding marks must run lengthwise for a focused arc.
Dedicated wheel = no cross-contamination from steel.
practice_problems.explanation: Tungsten preparation directly affects arc stability. Well-prepared = focused, stable. Contaminated = erratic, wandering.
distractors: Run at high amperage to clean it

practice_problems.question: A job requires TIG welding 3mm 304 stainless to 3mm mild steel (dissimilar metals). What filler rod and technique do you use? Why is filler selection critical?
practice_problems.answer: Filler: ER309L (over-alloyed austenitic). The higher Cr/Ni content compensates for dilution from the mild steel side. If 308L were used, dilution could produce brittle martensite. Technique: DCEN, pure argon. Direct 60-70% of heat toward the STAINLESS side (lower thermal conductivity needs more heat). If equal heat, mild steel melts excessively while stainless has lack-of-fusion. Use lower amperage to control dilution. Interpass temp <150°C. The mild steel HAZ may need stress relief for critical work.
hints: Key challenge is DILUTION: the pool mixes both base metals.
ER309L is the universal choice for stainless-to-carbon dissimilar joints.
practice_problems.explanation: Dissimilar metal TIG welding is advanced with high SA demand in food processing, mining, and petrochemical.
distractors: Cannot weld stainless to mild steel
Apply equal heat to both sides

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for TIG Welding (GTAW)
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
